topic: Ethics: Issues of Human Life & Death / Animal Life & Death
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: What does the doctrine of double effect permit at the end of life?
items.correct: It can permit giving pain relief that foreseeably shortens life, provided death is not intended and not the means of relieving pain, and there is proportionate reason.
items.misconception: The doctrine of double effect permits doctors to intentionally kill patients as long as the motive is compassionate.
items.random_wrong: The doctrine of double effect bans all pain relief for the dying.
items.why: Intention is the crux: the bad effect must be foreseen but unintended, and must not be the means to the good. Students turn DDE into a licence for euthanasia, which it explicitly rules out.

items.q: What does Singer mean by giving animals 'equal consideration'?
items.correct: Equal consideration of interests: like interests (e.g. avoiding suffering) count equally whichever species has them — which does not mean identical treatment for all species.
items.misconception: Singer claims animals and humans must always be treated in exactly the same way.
items.random_wrong: Singer argues animals matter more than humans.
items.why: Equal consideration is not equal treatment — a pig has no interest in voting. Students caricature Singer; his claim is that suffering counts the same regardless of the sufferer's species.

items.q: Where does Plato argue for immortality of soul?
items.why: It's Phaedo. “Republic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which doctrine is Catholic and Protestant disagreement on?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Purgatory. “Resurrection” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On which day did Jesus rise from the dead?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 day. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
